draft.introduction: Luke’s shorter version of the Lord’s Prayer, given in response to the disciples’ request “Lord, teach us to pray.” Its brevity compared to Matthew’s version raises questions about which form is more original.

translation.text: He was praying in a certain place, and when he finished, one of his disciples said to him, “Lord, teach us to pray, as John taught his disciples.” And he said to them, “When you pray, say: Father, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come. Give us each day our daily bread, and forgive us our sins, for we ourselves forgive everyone who is indebted to us. And lead us not into temptation.”
translation.original_text: πάτερ ἁγιασθήτω τὸ ὄνομά σου ἐλθέτω ἡ βασιλεία σου

translator_notes.note: Luke’s version begins simply “Father” (πάτερ) without Matthew’s “who is in heaven.” Most scholars consider Luke’s shorter form closer to the original. The bare address “Father” likely reflects the Aramaic Abba, an intimate form of address.
